item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only disappointment here is that it has so few upvotes compared to other great articles. Adding my +1 to help it along!

item.title: Re: Yo ho ho and a bottle of napalm
item.content:encoded: Personally this has been one of my favorite SCPs I have a love of navel combat and I am a sucker for ship on beast combat. Keep up the great work! I hope to see your work in the future.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Second image is from here, under Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Atlantic Rim. I would watch that movie! The idea was really interesting and you made it stand out. I'm not feeling the supernatural aspect so much, but I can just ignore it and the rest of the article makes up for it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the way you write this! It is really well written and I feel more people should know about this. Loved the way you threw in greek mythology there. Though I feel like there need to be a LOT more warships chasing down that monsters children before they grow up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding all this, also:
You call the captain Levi Hanson, but then call him Lyle Hanson a few paragraphs later. Which is it?

then Site-23 Director J. Calvin Coolidge

Calvin Coolidge died in 1933. Unless you're saying this was his son (in which case it should say J. Calvin Coolidge III).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Additional Errors/Fixes
– hide block
  • "SCP-2846-B is a large seafaring vessel that appears during SCP-2846-A appearance events. This vessel, which appears to be…" This is an opportunity to avoid redundant word usage.
  • "… it was agreed that the Foundation would make allowance to commandeer …" The clinical tone of this is frazzled.
  • "We spent the better part of the season hunting the creature, which at that point we believed to be some kind of whale, before we finally engaged the creature." Another opportunity to reduce redundancy.
  • "I… cannot say how long it was" (two sentences later:) "I cannot say for how long I walked that dark shore…" Another opportunity for redundancy.
  • "but in their arrogance they build Tartarus on Earth, endangering the lives of every human on earth." Not sure if it should be "built" or not, could be character speak I suppose.
  • Several "Foundation commands" may need to be capitalized for consistency.
  • "… one titan had escaped long ago… Calypso then told me that she feared that the titans who had escaped…" I am confused as to whether or not this is a typo, and if "titans" should be singular.
Questions
– hide block
  • "This vessel, which appears to be a Pennsylvania-Class super-dreadnought battleship…" Perhaps unintentionally, the wording of this comes off as ambiguity on the part of the Foundation. We are told that the Foundation gives SCP-B1 this ship, which SCP-B1 modifies, so why would they have ambiguity over what kind of ship it is? It's either the case that the Foundation is inexplicably confused about what ship they gave SCP-B1 or that the previous SCP-B was also a Pennsylvania-Class, or a look-alike. But if the latter is the case, it begs the questions: (1) how is the commandeered ship considered an upgrade from an apparently identical ship, (2) how did SCP-B1 have access to one prior to the Foundation's involvement, and (3) why then did SCP-B1 need to ask for their help?
  • Why is the Foundation commandeering a US-built ship when it could produce one itself, and arguably one more tailored to the mission? They would also avoid the need to expunge the entirety of the USS Atlanta's production records and the unspoken use of amnestics on those involved.
  • What is the point of mentioning the incendiary device aboard if it is simply, and immediately as far as the composition goes, replaced by a nuke?
  • If SCP-A can be blasted by guns off of a ship, why can't it be blasted with similar artillery (e.g. torpedoes) from installments around the site of SCP-C to prevent it from making more progress?
  • Who is the "they" in "If this is what they're saying…"?
  • What is the cover story given for the evacuation of Nassau?
My issues
– hide block
  • "Kensington-Berryman high-power transmitting device"; This is unnecessary and has no consequence in the article.
  • Striking out SCPS Pristine, and so many times, plays your hand too early. Would be nicer imo going into the incident logs not knowing what the fate of the ship will be.
  • "in the event of hostile action by SCP-2846-B" SCP-B as a possibly hostile entity is not seriously entertained in the article. If they perceived it to possibly be hostile, the hailing and boarding becomes ill-advised.
  • The detonation of a nuclear device in response to SCP-B becoming hostile to nearby civilian and Foundation ships seems hyper-reflexive, and a bad idea.
  • "Navy deep-sea radar wells to more accurately detect the appearance of SCP-2846-A, though this has only provided, on average, an additional 5 minutes of preparation." I don't see the purpose in including this.
  • "Knights of Truth website" I'm not sure what this is doing here.
  • It's odd that a multitude of individuals all used the same word, "vaporous", to describe SCP-B.
  • "information confirming this is scarce" If it's been confirmed, why does it matter that source material is scarce?
  • The article needlessly introduces "lyngbakr". This could be avoided by just using excerpts of the quoted passage.
  • "David Thomas Jones" "And you're aware of your…?" There is nothing inherently wrong with this. But I facepalmed. This is the point for me that the article starts to feel like an unbidden Pirates of the Caribbean installment.
  • Calypso comes off as awkwardly-placed narrative propellant and is discarded as haphazardly as she is included in the first place, especially considering her abilities. This is a goddess who used to run with Poseidon, and who can make a crew immortal, but can't do it again or multiple times so as to provide the support (or the bigger ship) that SCP-B1 needs to do the job, nor can she muster up any other supernatural means in general by which to help humanity. She feels like fan-service; as does Davey Jones, the Flying Dutchman, and Poseidon (who, like Calypso, is just gone).
  • To echo another criticism; the dialogue between the Cpt. and SCP-B1 is unnatural and filled with leading, narratively-forced questions (e.g. "This is all very well, but that still does not answer why you hailed us today.").
  • Another echo: the incident logs were tedious to read through. I appreciate the character of the reports as faithful to reality, but it makes for incredibly dry reading that is a slog, only to have happen what the reader expects.
  • The kamikaze nuking of the Pristine doesn't sit well with me because of their ~10 mile proximity to an island with a population of ~8,000. Having said that, I don't know a lot about nukes.
  • The Foundation evacuates Nassau (~ 118 km away) and not the island 16 km away.
  • "I don't want to say that there's probably an XK under there, but there's probably an XK under there." This line could be right at home spoken by the comedic relief in a hopeful summer blockbuster. The assumptions being made here are very large jumps in logic based on the observations, especially given the source, which is a creation-myth.
  • I don't think the [DATA EXPUNGED] works.

The -J page says as a caveat:

Just as with main series SCPs about mythology or folklore, it generally won't work unless you add a healthy dose of Foundation-flavored twist to it.

This is exactly what is happening here. The Foundation-flavor in this article is a garnish at best. It is a hodgepodge of mythology and the Foundation's reporting template.

item.title: Great job.
item.content:encoded: While this is technically a necro, I'd just like to say that the news network in the photo is FOX 40, which operates Sacramento, California. Which means that the foundation does, infact, operate in the US. Probably due to the fact that this page claims that the expunged news station is "local news"

item.title: Good scp
item.content:encoded: This scp is one of the better ones I see, although the way the interview log is sorted out. Other than that, good job!

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show
- Hide

To link to other content featuring Tau-11, I propose the following edit:

Mobile Task Force Tau-11 "Can Openers" onboard the SCPS Pristine SCPS Nikolai are to maintain a perimeter around active SCP-2846 activation areas.

to

Mobile Task Force Tau-11 "Can Openers" onboard the SCPS Pristine SCPS Nikolai are to maintain a perimeter around active SCP-2846 activation areas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, I love this one. It's a battleship duking it out with a Kraken, with Calypso and Davy Jones thrown in for good measure. My only question is, couldn't the Foundation use antisubmarine aircraft to track it? That seems a lot more efficient than what they're doing.

+1

item.title: Re: _Songs to listen to while reading this skip_
item.content:encoded: Na. This.

item.title: _Songs to listen to while reading this skip_
item.content:encoded: The entire soundtrack of the "Pirates of the Carribean" that's it, that's all you need

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there anything in the article that identifies the ship SCP-2846-B was before the USS Atlanta?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing sense of continuity and character development. The "we need a bigger boat" part sentsent chills down my spine.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why hasn't anybody written a tale crossover between this and SCP-2406?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Levian34 covered my biggest issue quite eloquently. Something clarifying they sat down with a recorder would help with the confusion there. I understand Capt. Jones was a bit rushed, but it comes off more as exposition and less…natural.

BUT…I adored this. You've managed to weave some of my favorite nautical myths into the Seal of the Titans. That alone deserves +1.

Picturing the Flying Dutchman as a Battleship: Priceless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here, have my first ever upvote.

I enjoyed the story, I'm a bit of a sucker for kraken stories and this was a good one. I especially enjoyed the weaving in of Greek mythology, it gave it gravity.

As for criticism, I found the engagement logs a bit tedious to read as they were monotone. I also found the reference to the "Flying Dutchman" gratingly obvious, but I guess others like that.

Looking forward to reading your other stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I stumbled across this looking to write a Kraken-esque SCP myself, but it looks like you beat me to it, so I suppose in lieu of writing my own I'll pitch in my two cents here.

I really enjoyed this one! I especially liked the inclusion of news footage and the incident log, it really rounds out the SCP.

However, I thought Addendum 2846.2 (The interview with Capt. Jones) was awkward.
I notice that the block is labeled and written as an interview, but the introduction of it seems to indicate something else. If the conversation is supposed to be a formal interview (in a room, sitting in chairs, with person writing everything down) than I would not change a thing about it. It is perfect. I would simply make it clearer that after Jones came aboard, Hanson conducted an interview with Jones.
However, it would follow from reading that Jones walks on deck and he and Capt. Hanson have a conversation right there. If this is the case, then I would suggest serious revision of the dialog to something more conversational and less narrative. This is the situation I imagined when I read it and it felt like very chunky and forced exposition especially on the part of Jones. Sort of "'Why are you here?' 'Let me tell you my life story!'". Not to say that the exposition was not interesting or important, but the delivery was off. Neither of the persons felt like realized people with personalities who were having a conversation. Some of the questions asked by the characters didn't seem like they were important, necessary, or appropriate to their context (e.g. the captain probably would not ask "Could you describe this woman?" and anyway, Jones would have likely described here anyway if he had mentioned her). But again, this is all I assuming the conversation happened right on deck.
Also, “David Thomas Jones” made me cringe a little. But then I giggled and decided that I liked it. So… there’s that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? Well, I was planning to have my SCP be extensive as yours because I think that's the way to go. Or that's the way I feel most comfortable. Thanks for telling me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A couple hours? I'm pretty sure I only had a handful of people critique it before it went up. Made some changes after the fact based on additional feedback, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 I really liked this SCP. The hook was good, and everything about this went very well and had a great story. I wish I could up vote this twice. BTW, how long did it take you to critique this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But dude. Pirate-Ghost jet fighters. ;-;

item.title: Re: Yo ho ho and a bottle of napalm
item.content:encoded:
The crew reminded me of One Piece
specifically, this bit

Because SPOILERS when a group of music-loving pirates are floating lost and they know they're all going to die from a disease that everyone has, what does the crew do? They sing their favorite song. They go down with smiles, in the good company of friends, because music is why they joined and music is how they'll leave the world. I cried. And I don't even follow One Piece.

This article reminded me of that. Good work, kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2846-B1: Aye. Calypso told me that she was the goddess of the sea, protector of its depths, mother of the waves. She told me that, in eons past, she and the god Neptune had gone into the deep of the ocean where the leviathans had made their domain, and banished them into the pit of Tartarus that their father, Uranus, had once crawled out from.

asdfghjkl; /mashes upvote button because MYTHOLOGYYYY

Also I felt sad at the end because ships. I was rooting for them. Calamari the squid. Avenge the fallen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other useless (patriotic) historical fact: the William heartlessed is talking about was Dutch Stadtholder William III. We owned the English for a little bit while we were sorting out this whole Catholics vs Protestants business. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay this is going to sound really nitpicky because I really like this article but also the inner history nerd in me is raging hard. In the interview, David Jones said he was "commissioned by the queen" to battle the kraken and set sail in 1685. The thing is, in 1685 England is ruled by a king-James II. And before that, another king, Charles II. If you moved the date forward a few years though, England will have Queens, in both Mary II（of which College of William & Mary) is named after, and later Queen Anne. Now that I have dispensed my useless historical fact, I shall retreat once more into the shame corner. Good day!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Insight: that's what it was originally going to be. That being said, I've already written a naval skip that uses a picture of an aircraft carrier, and I didn't want to do it twice.

Besides, battleships and destroyers have much larger guns than aircraft carriers. Aircraft carriers mostly just… carrier aircrafts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wondered if anybody would catch that ;D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a fun question: What would they do with an aircraft carrier?

Let's face it, we all want to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Time to sink a prototype navy ship loaded with experimental railguns for the good Captain! And pour concrete on the XK.

P.S. Liked the Coolidge throwaway for the Site Director.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A ship-of-the-line with black flags and billowing smoke in its wake.

Deathsworn! Oathbound!
On a quest for vengeful slaughter
We sail to the halls of doom,
With the black flag raised in the sky!

+1

item.title: Re: + 1'd
item.content:encoded: As a general rule, it's better to black out stuff that actually makes sense to black out; not for the sake of enhancing mystery… because it doesn't. A person with an average sense of intuition is more than capable of filling in the blanks, thereby redaction is a tool to engage the audience not exclude them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually disappointing, since I thought they were a clever pastiche. No wonder they seem unoriginal. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those myth descriptions are direct quotes of actual myths describing the Kraken.

item.title: Re: + 1'd
item.content:encoded: Gotta strongly disagree there. Blacking that wouldn't actually help add any mystery — it would just make it seem more generic. Part of the success of this SCP is that it has a lot of very specific stuff.

item.title: + 1'd
item.content:encoded: I really liked it, a lot of lore and documents here, well written. Maybe try blacking out the "Gulf-Atlantic region " part, or even just Gulf- Atlantic. Probably black out the last two numbers in 1685 in the interview. There is little mystery surrounding it, and everything is seen, but it's fine the way it is unless you want a bit of a mystery element.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh… the first part of it didn't grab me, but I was won over in the logs. I feel like there could be more done with this — we get a lot of detail that isn't really necessary or interesting and could be cut — but I still like it as it is, so +1.

EDIT: I just feel like you could do so much more with so much of this. Why do you only have stuff like the 'countless eyes' in the one log? Why all the rather generic (though well written) myth descriptions? Why not go a little crazier in those descriptions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite all the references to "Pirates of the Caribbean" (Kraken, Davy Jones-esque character, Calypso1), you pretty much sold me in on the whole concept and the ideas developed so well that it redeemed the somewhat blatant references.

I'm somewhat saddened that "Calypso" didn't really come into play in the article, other than a mysterious recruiter.

Footnotes
1. The mythological counterpart was only a nymph trapped on an island by the gods, unless you're referring to the Oceanid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is one of the best that I have read in along time. I just love how the Foundation is working with 1 half of the skip to fight the other half, good change of pace. I also have a soft spot for sea based skips. + 1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To say this SCP hooked me isn't enough. This captivated me. the pictures bring this together and the story is great. It had me interested from beginning to end. That doesn't happen often to me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want to say that there's probably and XK under there, but there's probably an XK under there.

Best way to break the news to someone.

Like others said, I'm also getting a big Pirates of the Caribbean vibe from this, but I don't really think it detracts from the SCP too much. I like the historical documents and the naval logs you added, plus the fact that 2846-B is aware of its situation and cooperative helps the overall feel of the article. But really, giant underwater monster, that basically sold me from the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well you've managed to one-up me on the same concept. Thanks for making me depressed. Again.

Returns to the bar to drink off his depression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ A critique/suggestion which is a bit wordy so I'm collapsing it
- Dance a little tune in the light of the black moon

The SCP as a whole is good, but I'm not liking the Davy Jones-Flying Dutchman-Calypso thing. It's too reminiscent of Pirates of the Caribbean.
Other than in the aforementioned film series, Davy Jones is a nebulous seafaring boogeyman that varies from story to story and often is not human or even formerly so (and sometimes more of a concept than a discrete entity).
The Flying Dutchman is so named because it is supposedly a ghostly Dutch man-o-war capable of flight (an ability probably originating from sightings of mirages of other ships).
The names make the -B aspect feel tongue-in-cheek to me, though a more oblique approach could keep the conceit you have with the names while toning that feeling down. Perhaps the original ship could be a Dutch man-o-war of indeterminate name (since you don't currently identify the type of ship) and the captain either Dutch with a Dutch name or, if you wish him to remain British, a privateer who commandeered the ship. The captain's name being "David Jones" would still be meh to me, though you might have him identify himself just as Captain Jones initially and perhaps reveal his first name in an off-hand way later. If he were Dutch, you could have him be named David Jonasen which could be easily conflated to "David Jones" without being too blatant.

In general I like it, but I'm no-voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have an issue with sea monsters but, why do they use seemingly regular old cannons to kill the thing? At the least they would use some kind of specialized ammunition type for neutralizing large, organic targets.

Also what about missiles or guided torpedoes?

Also wouldn't the foundation just toss a bunch of sonar buoys about the area to constantly keep tabs on it since it shows up on sonar?

Lastly, "Flying Dutchman". Got a kick out of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My solitary objection is that we're starting to get up there in terms of "monster pursued by vehicle of some sort" SCPs — at least, that's the impression that I get. The rest of the article, though, really made it stand out. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main problem with this is that's basically a Priates of the Caribbean movie in reverse. You've got a kraken, you've got Calypso, with Davy Jones as the good guy this time. (I also could have done without the historical documents going "Hey it's a kraken you guys".) But I upvoted in the end because you built up the ship logs very well. Skating on thin ice, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I am just a sucker for aquatic skips and naval warfare but this is one of my favorite recent SCPs.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now there are two different SCP's with massive potential for destruction named Iapetus; this, and the Saturnian moon Iapetus.

No correlation to the two, but, geez, is that a huge coincidence or what?

Also, +1 for SQUEEEEED.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I Really liked this one!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly because Rosemont was an actual ship that wasn't decommissioned before I have them absconding with it.

That being said, I don't know if I much like Orion either, so I'll probably change that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DAAAAAAHVEY JONESH' LOCKAAAAAARRR!

The images are much improved, though I'm curious- why the name change to Orion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That… that's actually a really good suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest issue is the unfortunate format limitation of the interview log. It's rather clunky, and a tale would do much more justice to it, but the stinger is still clever.

That aside, excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it in chat, liked it here. Plus I'm always happy to see MTF Tau-11 fighting another oceanic monster.

+1

item.title: Yo ho ho and a bottle of napalm
item.content:encoded:
This is probably the longest I've worked on a single idea, and I'm pretty pleased with how it's turned out. If I would've been around for the 1000 contest, this would've been my entry.

Anyway, thanks to everybody who commented and critiqued (there are so many of you please forgib), and as usual, additional comments, critiques and criticisms are welcome!
